title: Soy jar candle — bergamot and vetiver
subtitle: Citrus brightness grounded with a deep, smoky base
excerpt: A soy-wax container candle scented with bergamot and vetiver essential oils. Bergamot gives the first impression; vetiver deepens as the candle warms. A clean, grounding blend that works through the year.
sourceNotes: Soy wax fragrance load guidance: 6 to 8% of wax weight for container soy, documented in National Candle Association guidelines and Bramble Berry candlemaking notes. Essential oil dosing safety: Tisserand and Young, Essential Oil Safety (2nd ed., 2014). Bergamot flash point above 60°C; vetiver flash point above 100°C; both suitable for soy container work.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 jar candle (~200 ml jar, approximately 30 hours burn time)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale wax and fragrance weights proportionally per jar. Use one wick per jar regardless of batch size.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow at least 24 hours to cure before lighting. Two weeks of cure improves fragrance throw significantly.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Soy candles keep indefinitely if stored away from direct sunlight and heat. The fragrance oil can fade after 2 years; the wax itself does not go off. Discard if the wax shows visible mould (rare but possible in very damp storage).

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which an essential or fragrance oil can ignite if exposed to a flame. Oils with a flash point above the pour temperature are safe to add to candle wax; those with a flash point below the pour temperature risk volatilising before the wax sets.
The first burn of a new jar candle, which sets the melt pool. Burning until the entire surface has melted prevents the candle from tunnelling down the centre on subsequent burns.
The resting period after pouring, during which the fragrance oil binds fully with the wax matrix. Soy candles improve noticeably with 1 to 2 weeks of cure before first lighting.

projectSchedule.title: Fully set
projectSchedule.body: After 24 hours the wax is fully opaque and set. The candle can be lit from this point but benefits from longer cure.

projectSchedule.title: Optimal fragrance throw
projectSchedule.body: Two weeks of cure gives the best cold throw (scent when unlit) and hot throw (scent when burning). The bergamot-vetiver blend will have mellowed slightly from the sharper note at pouring.

content.text: Bergamot and vetiver is a pairing that works because the two essential oils sit at opposite ends of the volatility spectrum. Bergamot is a citrus top note, light and fresh; vetiver is a slow-releasing base note with a smoky, almost earthy quality. In a soy wax candle the bergamot comes through at cold throw, and vetiver deepens and dominates as the wax pool warms. Both oils have flash points comfortably above the pour temperature of 60 to 65°C for container soy wax, so they are added safely before pouring. The 7% fragrance load (7 g of essential oil per 100 g of wax) sits within the standard soy container range of 6 to 8%.

items.prepNote: Container blend soy wax; fills a 200 ml jar with standard shrinkage
CD-12 or equivalent for a 200 ml jar; size to jar diameter

content.text: Warm the jar gently (place in a low oven at 50°C for 5 minutes, then remove) to prevent frosting when the hot wax contacts a cold vessel. Secure the wick tab to the centre of the jar base with a small dot of wax or a wick sticker. Thread the wick through the centring bar and rest the bar across the top of the jar to hold the wick vertical.

Weigh the soy wax into the pouring pitcher and melt over a water bath or low direct heat. Do not leave melting wax unattended; wax above its flashpoint catches like an oil fire and water makes it worse. Keep a fire blanket within reach. Once fully melted, remove from heat and let the wax cool to 60 to 65°C, monitoring with the probe thermometer. At 60 to 65°C, add the bergamot and vetiver essential oils and stir for 30 seconds to combine evenly. Check the
of any essential oil before adding: it must be above the pour temperature.

Pour the scented wax slowly into the prepared jar, keeping the wick centred. Leave a 1 cm gap at the top. Do not move the jar for at least 4 hours while the wax sets. A small sink hole may form around the wick as the wax cools; top up with a small pour of wax heated to the same temperature. Allow the candle to cure for at least 24 hours before lighting. Two weeks of
Trim the wick to 5 mm before each lighting. On the first burn, allow the wax to melt all the way to the edge of the jar before extinguishing; this establishes the
and prevents the candle tunnelling on subsequent lightings. A 200 ml jar typically needs 3 to 4 hours for the first burn.

Store unlit candles away from direct sunlight (which bleaches soy wax) and away from heat sources. The wax itself lasts indefinitely. The fragrance note fades after 2 years in storage; the bergamot top note will go first, leaving primarily the vetiver base. Cap the jar when not in use to protect the cold throw.
